title: Comparative analysis of nonlinear elastic moduli of polystyrene, polycarbonate and PMMA
authors: A. V. Belashov, A. A. Zhikhoreva, Y. M. Beltukov, I. V. Semenova
abstract: We present the comparative experimental analysis of frequency dependencies of linear (Lamé) and nonlinear (Murnaghan) elastic moduli of polystyrene, PMMA and polycarbonate. The measurement methodology, based on the acousto-elastic effect, provided data on variations of these moduli in block samples of the polymers in the frequency range of 0.45-3 MHz. In all the three polymers the linear Lamé moduli demonstrated moderate rise with frequency, most pronounced rise was observed in modulus $λ$ of PMMA in about 35%. The frequency dependencies of Murnaghan moduli were considerably nonlinear. At higher frequencies above ~1 MHz no significant variations of the Murnaghan moduli occurred, while at lower frequencies the absolute values of the moduli $l$ and $m$ demonstrated rapid rise, more pronounced for the modulus $l$. At the same time the absolute values of the modulus $n$ decreased and demonstrated a tendency to become positive at lower frequencies. Both linear and nonlinear moduli of PMMA had higher values than those of PC and PS, with the latter two demonstrating close values of both types of moduli. The potential origins of the differences in nonlinear elastic properties of the three polymers are discussed.
summary: This paper provides a comparative experimental analysis of the linear and nonlinear elastic properties of three common glassy polymers: PS, PC, and PMMA. Using ultrasonic wave measurements, the authors demonstrate that nonlinear moduli are much more sensitive to frequency changes than linear moduli, particularly below 1 MHz. The study identifies that PMMA's polar groups lead to higher moduli values and highlights how molecular structures influence relaxation processes. The findings help validate nonlinear viscoelastic models for amorphous materials.
main_problem: The study addresses the lack of experimental data regarding the frequency and temperature dependence of nonlinear viscoelastic properties in glassy amorphous polymers.
main_result: Nonlinear elastic moduli exhibit much more profound frequency dependence (1.5-2.5x) than linear moduli, with significant variations occurring below 1 MHz due to relaxation processes. PMMA showed higher absolute values for both linear and nonlinear moduli compared to PC and PS.
method: The researchers used the acousto-elastic effect to measure variations in longitudinal and shear ultrasonic wave velocities under applied static pressure (0-12 MPa) in the frequency range of 0.4-3 MHz.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of three glassy amorphous polymers: Polystyrene (PS), Polycarbonate (PC), and Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). The mechanical behavior is modeled using Murnaghan's model for nonlinear elasticity, which includes five parameters (two Lamé and three Murnaghan moduli) derived from the energy density function.
key_observables: Longitudinal and shear ultrasonic wave velocities, wave packet transition times, and phase shifts under static pressure.
important_parameters: Lamé moduli (lambda, mu), Murnaghan moduli (l, m, n), frequency range (0.4-3 MHz), and the relaxation exponent (gamma between 2.4 and 2.9).
main_assumptions: The polymers are assumed to have a broad spectrum of relaxation times; the applied pressure range is assumed to be within the elastic regime without irreversible deformation; and the effective elastic moduli are assumed to depend linearly on static pressure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 is a flowchart of data processing; Figure 3 displays frequency dependence of wave velocities; Figure 4 shows molecular structures of the polymers; Figure 5 shows frequency dependence of moduli.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear viscoelasticity, describes the experimental setup and materials, details the mathematical models for calculating moduli, presents the frequency-dependent results and material comparisons, and concludes with a discussion on the molecular origins of the observed nonlinearities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
